g.data-narration: Welcome to Test of Significance - the foundation for making data-driven decisions in engineering and computer science.
g.text: Test of Significance: Statistical Inference Foundation

g.data-narration: A test of significance is a systematic procedure that helps us determine whether observed data supports a claim or is due to random chance.
g.text: WHAT IS A TEST OF SIGNIFICANCE?
differences are real or due to random chance.

g.data-narration: In computer science, we use these tests for A/B testing algorithms, validating software performance, and ensuring data-driven decisions are statistically sound.
g.text: WHY IT MATTERS IN COMPUTER SCIENCE
• Data Science & ML: Evaluate model metrics

g.data-narration: Every test begins with two competing hypotheses: the null hypothesis (no effect) and the alternative hypothesis (the claim we want to prove).
g.text: status quo. Always stated as equality.
The claim we want to test for:

g.data-narration: Alternative hypotheses can be one-sided (testing for improvement in one direction) or two-sided (testing for any difference in either direction).
g.text: H₁: μ > μ₀ or H₁: μ < μ₀

g.data-narration: The test statistic measures how far our sample data deviates from the null hypothesis, standardized by the standard error.
The significance level (alpha) is our threshold for rejecting the null hypothesis - typically 0.05 (5%) meaning 95% confidence.
g.text: Threshold set BEFORE the test. Defines risk of incorrectly
rejecting a TRUE null hypothesis (Type I Error).

g.data-narration: The p-value is the probability of obtaining results as extreme as observed, assuming the null hypothesis is true. A smaller p-value means stronger evidence against H₀.
g.text: Probability of observing data as extreme as (or more extreme than)
what we got, assuming H₀ is TRUE.

g.data-narration: We compare p-value to alpha to make our decision: if p-value is less than or equal to alpha, we reject the null hypothesis.
Let's walk through a complete example: testing if a server's mean response time differs from the claimed 50 milliseconds.
g.text: Problem: Server claims μ = 50ms. Sample: n=100, X̄=53ms, σ=10ms. Test at α=0.05

g.data-narration: We can make two types of errors: Type I (false positive - rejecting true H₀) controlled by alpha, and Type II (false negative - failing to reject false H₀).
g.text: Fail to reject H₀ when FALSE

g.data-narration: Key takeaways: We test claims using null and alternative hypotheses, calculate a test statistic, compare p-value to alpha, and make decisions about the data.
Apply this knowledge to A/B testing, algorithm comparisons, performance validation, and data science experiments in your career.
Thank you for learning about Test of Significance. Practice with real data to master this essential statistical tool for evidence-based decision making.
